title: Second-Order Effects Planning

content.statement: The consequence after the consequence often matters more than the first-order win.
content.question: If your business hits its launch numbers perfectly, what does that create that you haven't planned for?
content.equation: SOEP = First-Order Gain (rate 1-10) + Positive Second-Order Effects (list each one, rate 1-10, sum them) - Negative Second-Order Effects (list each one, rate 1-10, sum them)
sop: Take your current top initiative and write down the first-order goal, what success literally looks like.
List every second-order consequence you can think of, both good (new opportunities) and bad (new costs, new competitors, new complexity).
Rate your First-Order Gain 1-10, then rate each Positive and Negative Second-Order Effect 1-10 and sum them separately (see calculation, page 6)
For every negative second-order effect you identified, decide now what you'll do if it materializes, don't wait until it happens.
Revisit this list 60 days after the initiative launches and check which second-order effects actually showed up.
content.explainer: Winning the first-order battle can lose the second-order war. Always ask "and then what?"
questions: If your business hits its launch numbers perfectly, what does that create that you haven't planned for?
What's a second-order effect of your last big win that you're only now dealing with?
If your current growth plan works exactly as intended, what new problem does success itself create?

definitions.def: Asking "and then what?" after a decision.

content.equation_example: Rate First-Order Gain 1-10, then list your anticipated second-order effects and rate each 1-10. First-Order Gain 8; Positive Second-Order Effects: new referrals 7 + press interest 5 = 12; Negative Second-Order Effects: support overload 6 = 6. SOEP = 8 + 12 - 6 = 14. Higher SOEP means the ripple effects of this win work in your favor; lower or negative SOEP means the second-order costs could outweigh the first-order gain.
content.closing_statement: Winning the first move can lose the next one.

caption:
Principle Nº 37: Second-Order Effects Planning

The consequence after the consequence often matters more than the first-order win. Winning the first-order battle can lose the second-order war.

Ask yourself: If your business hits its launch numbers perfectly, what does that create that you haven't planned for?
